title: DeonticBench: A Benchmark for Reasoning over Rules
authors: Guangyao Dou, Luis Brena, Akhil Deo, William Jurayj, Jingyu Zhang, Nils Holzenberger, Benjamin Van Durme (Johns Hopkins University; Télécom Paris, Institut Polytechnique de Paris)
venue: arXiv preprint, submitted 6 April 2026, cs.CL, v1. Formatted with a colm2026_conference.sty style file (suggests submission/target of COLM 2026) but no COLM acceptance is confirmed by the arXiv listing itself — flagged as an unresolved venue ambiguity in the local extraction header.
claim: LLMs struggle to reliably perform 'deontic reasoning' -- applying obligations/permissions/prohibitions from long, explicit statutes and case facts -- across four high-stakes real-world domains (U.S. federal tax, airline baggage fees, U.S. immigration appeals, U.S. state housing law), even when given an optional symbolic (Prolog) solver-assisted workflow. The Abstract states best hard-subset performance reaches only 44.4% accuracy on SARA Numeric and 46.6 macro-F1 on Housing; these two figures are the best scores among the 8 general-purpose LLMs in the main results table (O3 Zero-Shot and GPT-4.1 Few-Shot respectively) -- note that within that same main table GPT-5.1 Few-Shot actually scores higher on Housing (46.8), and the separately-reported coding model GPT-5.2-Codex scores higher on SARA Numeric (45.8, Zero-Shot, Appendix C.1), so the Abstract's 'best...only 44.4%/46.6' framing is not literally the maximum across all 11 evaluated models. Supervised fine-tuning plus reinforcement learning on a 32B open model improves classification tasks but not numeric reasoning, leaving reliable end-to-end rule-grounded reasoning unsolved.
novelty: The authors claim three contributions: (1) DeonticBench, described as 'the largest benchmark to date for executable rule-based reasoning in real-world, high-stakes domains' (6,232 tasks combining three existing domains -- SARA/tax, Airline/RuleArena, Housing -- with a newly constructed 242-case USCIS-AAO immigration-appeals dataset), unified into one input-output task format with released reference Prolog programs for every instance; (2) a comprehensive evaluation of 8 general-purpose LLMs (5 reasoning models) and 3 coding models revealing that all models struggle on curated 'hard' subsets and that increased reasoning effort does not consistently help; (3) an investigation of SFT/DPO/GRPO training on Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct showing training improves Prolog-generation quality but current RL methods still fail to solve the tasks reliably. Vs. RuleArena/CL-bench (named as closest prior work), the paper claims DeonticBench is larger (6,232 vs. 816 tasks), covers additional high-stakes legal domains, and adds executable Prolog artifacts for program-trace analysis (Related Work §5.1).

protocol.description: Each DeonticBench instance is scored via ONE model call under one of three prompting strategies -- Direct, Zero-Shot, or Few-Shot (§3.1) -- there is no multi-turn interaction loop or iterative feedback to the model during scoring. The paper is explicit that Zero-shot/Few-shot 'generates a complete executable Prolog program from statutes and case facts... for automatic execution and scoring' in a single generation, and Direct 'predicts the final answer without generating Prolog' in a single generation. The only place multi-turn re-prompting occurs is in the offline DATASET-CONSTRUCTION pipeline (not the evaluated protocol): 'we allow up to two attempts per instance: on failure, we re-prompt with the prior code and SWI-Prolog feedback; if the second attempt fails, we discard the instance' (§2.4/§B.1) -- this is a data-curation step to build the released reference Prolog artifacts, not part of the evaluated model protocol.
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees the FULL problem in one shot, but the composition of that observation is NOT uniform across domains: for SARA Numeric/Binary, Airline, and USCIS-AAO, the model sees the complete statute text, a narrative case text, and the question/claim (Appendix B.4). E.g. SARA Numeric zero-shot template: '## Statutes: <statutes> ## Case: <case text> ## Question: <question> ... First, write a logic program in prolog to encode the relevant rules... Then write a logic program in prolog to encode the facts... Then write a query to compute and print the value.' Housing is the exception and lacks a case-text field entirely: per §E.5, 'Unlike the other three domains, Housing instances contain no narrative case description -- each instance is a (state, statutes, question) triple,' and the Housing Zero-Shot template (§B.4) takes only 'state', 'focus_year' (fixed at 2021), 'question', and 'statutes' -- no case-facts section. Since Housing is 5,314 of the 6,232 total tasks (85%), the 'statute + case + question' description is not representative of the majority of the benchmark's instances. Few-shot adds in-context exemplars (curated by humans, §2.4/§B.1: 'stratify accepted examples by domain, sample a small balanced set as few-shot demonstrations'). No partial per-turn feedback is given to the model at evaluation time -- the Prolog is generated once, then executed externally by SWI-Prolog and scored automatically. All prompts are preceded by a fixed system message: 'You are a helpful assistant trained to conduct deontic reasoning.' (§B.4).

representations.variants_compared: Two orthogonal representation axes are varied: (1) output-representation setting -- symbolic (model emits executable Prolog code that is compiled/run by SWI-Prolog) vs. non-symbolic/Direct (model emits the final answer directly in natural language, no code); (2) demonstration/prompting strategy -- Direct, Zero-Shot (Prolog, no exemplars), Few-Shot (Prolog, with exemplars). No alternative *input* serializations (e.g., JSON vs. prose statutes) are compared in the extraction.
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Effects are highly domain- and model-dependent with no single strategy dominating: 'few-shot helps Airline but not SARA Numeric, while zero-shot and direct solving often work better on binary tasks (SARA Binary, USCIS-AAO) than on Housing' (§4.1). Concrete swing example: Qwen3-235B rises from 0.7 (Few-Shot) to 32.1 (Direct) on SARA Numeric -- 'near-random few-shot performance... to 32.1 under direct prompting' (§4.1). Symbolic (Prolog) generation trades abstentions for correctness vs. Direct: 'when solving tasks via Prolog, models exhibit higher abstention rates, whereas direct prompting reduces abstentions but increases the frequency of incorrect predictions' (§4.1). No model/setting combination is best across all five task columns (Table: Main results, §2.6).

models.name: GPT-4.1
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 23.7 (Few-Shot), 6.7 (Zero-Shot), 18.8 (Direct); Airline Acc.: 41.5/1.7/6.7; SARA Bin. F1: 39.1/40.5/30.3; USCIS-AAO F1: 53.0/55.5/50.9; Housing F1: 46.6/44.7/20.2 (Table: Main results, §2.6).

models.name: O3
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 15.2 (Few-Shot), 44.4 (Zero-Shot -- highest SARA Numeric result among the 8 general-purpose LLMs, and the figure cited in the Abstract; note GPT-5.2-Codex, a coding model reported separately in Appendix C.1, scores higher at 45.8), 33.5 (Direct); Airline Acc.: 90.8/18.5/37.8; SARA Bin. F1: 29.5/32.3/59.5; USCIS-AAO F1: 49.4/48.5/52.6; Housing F1: 43.0/39.6/20.8 (Table: Main results, §2.6). Abstract: 'best hard-subset performance reaches only 44.4% on SARA Numeric.'

models.name: GPT-5.1
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 33.1/44.0/15.9; Airline Acc.: 52.3/40.2/28.4; SARA Bin. F1: 28.7/20.2/54.0; USCIS-AAO F1: 61.4/65.9/71.5 (Direct, highest USCIS-AAO); Housing F1: 46.8/41.2/18.4 (Table: Main results). Also: 'GPT-5.1 consistently outperforms GPT-5.2 across SARA Numeric, SARA Binary, USCIS-AAO, and Housing, and remains competitive on Airline' (§C.2).

models.name: GPT-5.2
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 17.1/27.6/20.7; Airline Acc.: 77.9/2.5/29.9; SARA Bin. F1: 24.3/16.0/25.8; USCIS-AAO F1: 37.6/51.5/58.3; Housing F1: 41.1/33.4/17.4 (Table: Main results). Used as one of three models for hard-set candidate identification (§B.2).

models.name: Kimi K2 Instruct (0905)
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 9.3/10.1/8.4; Airline Acc.: 42.6/0.0/0.9; SARA Bin. F1: 48.5/52.7/68.4 (Direct, highest SARA Binary among general LLMs alongside note in §4.1); USCIS-AAO F1: 38.2/43.4/51.8; Housing F1: 37.1/39.8/24.9 (Table: Main results). Cited in §4.1: 'competitive scores (66.5 on SARA Binary for Qwen3-235B and 68.4 for Kimi K2)' under Direct prompting.

models.name: Claude Sonnet 4.5
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 21.6/21.9/41.2 (Direct, third-highest SARA Numeric among general LLMs, behind O3's 44.4 and GPT-5.1's 44.0); Airline Acc.: 85.8/5.7/6.2; SARA Bin. F1: 28.7/43.9/70.0 (Direct, highest SARA Binary overall across all general and coding models); USCIS-AAO F1: 63.1/63.0/7.2 (large Direct-mode collapse); Housing F1: 42.9/45.0/32.1 (Table: Main results). Also used for hard-set candidate identification (§B.2).

models.name: Gemini 2.5 Flash
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 2.7/0.9/30.5; Airline Acc.: 28.4/0.6/18.1; SARA Bin. F1: 44.7/16.5/61.1; USCIS-AAO F1: 31.8/33.4/45.9; Housing F1: 43.4/46.0/30.2 (Table: Main results).

models.name: Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 0.7 (Few-Shot, 'near-random'), 8.7 (Zero-Shot), 32.1 (Direct); Airline Acc.: 22.9/4.6/12.8; SARA Bin. F1: 37.8/27.2/66.5 (Direct); USCIS-AAO F1: 24.4/35.8/53.1; Housing F1: 38.6/43.5/25.7 (Table: Main results, §4.1).

models.name: GPT-5.2-Codex (coding model)
models.key_results: SARA Num. Acc.: 26.6/45.8 (Zero-Shot, highest single SARA Numeric number in the whole paper)/29.5; Airline Acc.: 95.5 (Few-Shot, highest Airline score overall)/25.6/47.0; SARA Bin. F1: 28.8/16.6/36.5; USCIS-AAO F1: 34.9/47.4/52.7; Housing F1: 41.3/31.3/17.5 (Table: Results for coding LLMs, §C.1). 'GPT-5.2-Codex achieves the strongest overall performance, yet its performance remains sensitive to prompts' (§C.1).

models.name: Qwen3-Coder-Next-FP8 / Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct-FP8 (coding models)
models.key_results: Frequently near-zero, e.g. Qwen3-Coder-Next: Airline Acc. 0.0 across all three settings, SARA Num. 0.0/0.0/4.3 (Table: Results for coding LLMs, §C.1). 'Qwen-based coding models frequently fail the tasks, resulting in near-zero scores in several settings' (§C.1).

models.name: Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (base + SFT/DPO/GRPO variants)
models.key_results: Base model: SARA Num. Acc. 1.3/0.7/4.4; Airline 15.9/0.0/0.9. After SFT: USCIS-AAO Few-Shot F1 rises 10.3 -> 35.0 ('few-shot USCIS-AAO rises from 10.3 to over 45 with alignment-based training', §4.2, referring to DPO+SFT reaching 45.7). SARA Numeric stays below 10 across all training methods and prompting strategies (§4.2: 'SARA Numeric remains below 10 across methods and prompting strategies'). Best Airline result among trained models: GRPO+DPO+SFT Few-Shot 60.4 (Table: Results for locally trained models, §4.2).

headline_numbers: 6,232 total tasks across 4 domains (Abstract; confirmed verbatim in extraction self-check).
Best hard-subset performance reaches only 44.4% accuracy on SARA Numeric (O3, Zero-Shot) and 46.6 macro-F1 on Housing (GPT-4.1, Few-Shot) (Abstract; Table: Main results).
DeonticBench vs. RuleArena size comparison: 6,232 vs. 816 tasks (§5.1 Related Work).
Qwen3-235B swings from 0.7 (Few-Shot, near-random) to 32.1 (Direct) on SARA Numeric, illustrating open-source prompt sensitivity (§4.1).

Error-type breakdown on frontier-model failures (GPT-5.1/5.2/O3, hard cases failing all K generations): Housing errors are 96.8% (Few-Shot) / 93.5% (Zero-Shot) 'Wrong Rule'; Airline errors are 100% (Few-Shot) / 75% (Zero-Shot) 'Numerical'; USCIS-AAO Zero-Shot errors are 77.3% 'Wrong Rule' (Table: Distribution of error types, §4.3).

SFT/RL training on Qwen2.5-32B: SARA Numeric remains below 10 across all training methods/prompting strategies; USCIS-AAO Few-Shot F1 rises from 10.3 (base) to 45.7 (DPO+SFT) (§4.2, Table: Results for locally trained models).

Reasoning-effort ablation on SARA Numeric: 'performance does not consistently improve with higher reasoning effort; in some cases, additional effort leads to diminishing or even negative returns' (§C.2).
failure_modes: Four-category error taxonomy on hard cases where GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, and O3 failed every K generation (§4.3, Table: Distribution of error types): (1) Wrong Rule -- model applied wrong statute/subsection/fee schedule, dominant in Housing (96.8% Few-Shot, 93.5% Zero-Shot) and USCIS-AAO (42.6% Few-Shot, 77.3% Zero-Shot); (2) Entity/Fact -- correct rule but wrong facts encoded, dominant in SARA Numeric (42% Few-Shot, 52% Zero-Shot) and prominent in SARA Binary; (3) Numerical -- correct rule+facts but arithmetic/threshold errors, dominant in Airline (100% Few-Shot, 75% Zero-Shot); (4) Implementation Bugs -- low-level Prolog errors unrelated to reasoning, notable in USCIS-AAO (42.3% Few-Shot, 22.7% Zero-Shot). General conclusion: 'failures are domain-dependent: legal domains are bottlenecked by rule selection, structured reasoning tasks by fact extraction, and quantitative domains by arithmetic precision' (§4.3). Separately, a coverage/reliability trade-off is reported: symbolic (Prolog) generation raises abstention rates while Direct prompting lowers abstentions but raises incorrect-answer rates (§4.1). Coding models show prompt-sensitive catastrophic failures: 'small prompt variations leading to catastrophic failures' for Qwen-based coding models (§C.1). Wide bootstrap confidence intervals across hard subsets 'suggest[] variability beyond sample-size effects' (§4.1).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The EVALUATED protocol is entirely one-shot / single forward pass per instance: each of Direct, Zero-Shot, and Few-Shot prompting produces one output (natural-language answer or one Prolog program) that is scored without any interactive feedback loop to the model. The paper's only multi-step mechanism is external and offline: during reference-dataset CONSTRUCTION (not evaluation), a re-prompting loop with up to two attempts is used to raise the yield of curated Prolog artifacts, using compiler/runtime feedback -- 'on failure, we re-prompt with the prior code and SWI-Prolog feedback; if the second attempt fails, we discard the instance' (§2.4/§B.1) -- but this is not part of the model evaluation the headline numbers come from. The paper does not test an interactive/multi-turn evaluation condition, nor does it report a many-demonstration (beyond a small curated few-shot set) or algorithm-in-prompt condition distinct from the three prompting strategies. Its conclusion about capability across effort/demonstration axes is that neither more reasoning effort (§C.2) nor few-shot demonstration (§4.1, domain-dependent) reliably improves performance, and that post-hoc training (SFT/DPO/RL) 'improve[s] performance, but yielding limited gains in end-to-end task accuracy' (Abstract, §4.2) -- i.e. the paper finds no single axis (more inference-time reasoning, more demonstrations, or more training) reliably closes the gap in one forward pass or otherwise.

relation_to_candidate.position: DeonticBench sits at the opposite end of the family's protocol/output axes from c22. Protocol mode: single-shot only (Direct/Zero-Shot/Few-Shot are all one forward pass per instance; no interactive multi-turn evaluation loop, §3.1). Observation: the model sees the FULL problem context in one call with no partial/turn-wise feedback during evaluation, but the content of that context is domain-dependent -- for SARA/Airline/USCIS-AAO it is statute text plus a narrative case description plus question (per domain-specific template, §B.4), while Housing (5,314 of 6,232 tasks, 85% of the benchmark) has NO narrative case text at all: 'Housing instances contain no narrative case description -- each instance is a (state, statutes, question) triple' (§E.5). Output shape: for numeric/binary tasks, a single scalar or single-token label (a tax dollar amount, a bag-fee number, an Entailment/Contradiction or Accepted/Dismissed token) OR, in the symbolic setting, a full executable Prolog PROGRAM (a structured multi-line artifact, not a short label) that is then run by an external solver. Verifier type: an external, real symbolic interpreter (SWI-Prolog) that executes generated code and compares its printed output to a gold label with $1 tolerance (numeric) or exact class match (macro-F1 tasks) -- a genuine execution-based verifier, richer than a deterministic string/format checker, but coarser-grained: it verifies only the FINAL printed answer, with no per-sub-rule or per-checker breakdown surfaced to the scoring pipeline itself (the four-category error taxonomy in §4.3 is a post-hoc human/manual classification of failures, not a per-instance diagnostic the benchmark computes automatically).
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c22: Both are 'verifiable' benchmarks in the loose sense that scoring is deterministic/automatable rather than LLM-judged: DeonticBench via Prolog execution against a gold label, c22 via a checker-library boolean predicate per constraint. Both also involve a form of 'discover the applicable rule rather than being told it directly' -- DeonticBench's rule-selection errors (Housing 96.8% Wrong Rule, §4.3) parallel c22's Selection constraints where the model must infer which conditional branch (rule A vs B) applies from an unstated property of the input. Both report per-category/per-atom diagnostic breakdowns of what failed (DeonticBench's 4-way error taxonomy in Table: Distribution of error types vs. c22's per-checker diagnostic verdicts).
relation_to_candidate.differences: The two are otherwise far apart on essentially every structural axis the corpus catalogues: (1) input/output scale -- DeonticBench inputs run to thousands of tokens of statute text (mean 6,118 tokens for SARA statutes, Table: Statistics of datasets) and outputs are full Prolog programs (mean 361-1236 tokens, same table) or domain-grounded numeric/binary answers, whereas c22 is deliberately a 'trivial micro-task' with a 'few words' output; (2) verification granularity -- DeonticBench's verifier is a real-world execution engine checking a single final answer per instance (no built-in per-constraint decomposition at scoring time), while c22's checker library independently evaluates 3-5 constraints per instance with strict all-pass 0/1 and per-atom diagnostics logged natively; (3) scoring is not all-or-nothing for most DeonticBench splits -- 4 of 5 reported metrics are macro-F1 over a full evaluation set (partial-credit-like at the dataset level, even though each instance's Prolog answer is itself binary-correct/incorrect), versus c22's per-INSTANCE strict all-pass with no partial credit; (4) task domain and provenance -- DeonticBench draws on real, high-stakes legal/tax/regulatory corpora (curated, not synthetically generated, and finite/exhaustible: 6,232 fixed tasks) versus c22's synthetically generated, in-principle-infinite COLLIE-style grammar pool; (5) DeonticBench's 'optional Prolog verifier' is a heavyweight symbolic-execution artifact (compiled and run by SWI-Prolog, requiring a 20s timeout, with abstention/non-execution as its own failure category) versus c22's lightweight, pure-Python string/regex-style checkers with no execution environment needed.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: Relative to DeonticBench, c22 would add: (a) a much finer, per-atom automatic diagnostic signal built into scoring itself (DeonticBench's error taxonomy is a manual post-hoc human categorization over a sample of failures, not an automatic per-instance breakdown the benchmark computes at run time); (b) a synthetic, contamination-resistant, effectively infinite generation pool versus DeonticBench's fixed, finite, real-document-derived 6,232 tasks (already partially exposed via a public GitHub release, raising a contamination consideration that c22's synthetic-grammar design sidesteps); (c) a much cheaper/faster oracle (pure Python constraint checks vs. compiling and executing Prolog with a 20-second timeout per attempt); (d) an explicit, tunable incremental difficulty dial via constraint count/level (DeonticBench's difficulty is fixed by real-world domain complexity and a hand-curated hard-set, not an algorithmically adjustable dial); and (e) strict single-instance all-or-nothing scoring at the per-example level (DeonticBench mostly aggregates to macro-F1 across many binary-labeled instances, which is a different reliability target than c22's atomic 0/1-per-example scoring).

corpus_claim: DeonticBench (Dou et al., 2026) offers 6,232 rule-reasoning tasks with an optional Prolog verifier and explicit rule traces; a 2026 successor to the RuleArena rule-application family adjacent to c22.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The '6,232 rule-reasoning tasks' figure is verbatim-confirmed in the abstract and extraction self-check (line 35/23). The 'optional Prolog verifier' framing is supported: the benchmark 'enables an optional solver-based workflow' alongside 'free-form chain-of-thought reasoning' and a Direct (non-symbolic) setting that requires no Prolog at all (Abstract; §3.1). 'Explicit rule traces' is supported: the paper describes the Prolog workflow as providing 'a formal interpretation of the input and an explicit program trace' (§1 Introduction) and releases reference Prolog programs for all instances. However, the claim that DeonticBench is 'a successor to the RuleArena rule-application family' overstates the relationship as described in the paper: RuleArena is only ONE of DeonticBench's four source domains (the Airline subset is 'a subset from RuleArena', §2.2), not the lineage DeonticBench as a whole succeeds -- the other three domains (SARA/tax, Housing, USCIS-AAO) come from separate prior works (Holzenberger & Van Durme 2023; Zheng et al. 2025 legal-IR/RAG; and a wholly new USCIS-AAO dataset the authors constructed). The paper itself frames RuleArena (together with CL-bench) as merely 'the closest to our setting' for a size/scope comparison (§5.1: 'DeonticBench is larger (6,232 vs. 816 tasks in RuleArena)'), not as a direct predecessor DeonticBench formally succeeds. Calling it 'adjacent to c22' is a corpus-internal editorial framing not addressed by the paper itself (paper never mentions IFEval/IFBench/verifiable-constraint instruction-following at all), so that portion of the claim is neither supported nor contradicted by the paper -- it is the corpus author's own positioning.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials: DeonticBench curates domain-specific 'hard' subsets via automated filtering (run 3 frontier models -- O3, GPT-5.2, Claude 4.5 Sonnet -- with 2 runs each; any instance where at least one generation fails is 'potentially hard') plus iterative human validation (§B.2); hard-subset sizes are small and fixed: SARA Numeric hard=35, SARA Binary hard=30, Airline hard=80, Housing hard=78, USCIS-AAO hard=28 (Table: Statistics of datasets, §2.6).

Prompt format materially swings accuracy in a domain-dependent, non-monotonic way: 'few-shot helps Airline but not SARA Numeric, while zero-shot and direct solving often work better on binary tasks... than on Housing' (§4.1); open-source/coding models are especially prompt-sensitive, with catastrophic collapses to near-0 in some settings (Qwen3-Coder-Next Airline: 0.0 across all 3 settings, §C.1).

Metrics: accuracy with $1 tolerance for SARA Numeric/Airline (numeric tasks: |round(y) - int(y_hat)| <= 1, §B.3); macro-F1 for SARA Binary/Housing/USCIS-AAO binary tasks (§B.3); abstentions (non-executable Prolog or unparsable direct output) are always counted as incorrect (mapped to -1 for numeric or the opposite class for binary tasks, §B.3); bootstrap CIs use B=1000 replicates, K=3-4 samples per case to capture stochasticity (§B.3).

Floors/ceilings observed: overall ceiling is low -- best SARA Numeric is 45.8 (GPT-5.2-Codex, Zero-Shot, coding-model table, §C.1) / 44.4 (O3, Zero-Shot, general-model table, and the number cited in the Abstract) and best Housing across the general-model main table is 46.8 (GPT-5.1, Few-Shot) -- narrowly ahead of GPT-4.1's 46.6 (Few-Shot), which is the figure the Abstract actually cites; floor is nearly 0 for several open-source/coding models on Airline and SARA Numeric under mismatched prompting strategy (e.g., Qwen3-235B Few-Shot SARA Numeric 0.7, multiple coding-model Airline scores of 0.0, §4.1/§C.1); increasing reasoning effort does NOT reliably raise the ceiling ('performance does not consistently improve with higher reasoning effort; in some cases, additional effort leads to diminishing or even negative returns', §C.2).

Contamination/data-release status: the paper releases code and data publicly ('Code and data are available at https://github.com/guangyaodou/DeonticBench', Abstract footnote) including reference Prolog programs for all instances -- this is a FIXED, finite, publicly released dataset (unlike a synthetic infinite-pool design), so any future baseline built on DeonticBench-adjacent ideas should consider that its exact instances could appear in later model pretraining corpora; no explicit contamination analysis or decontamination procedure is reported in the extraction.

Training baselines for comparison: SFT/DPO/Dr.GRPO on Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct with LoRA (rank 8 for SFT/DPO, rank 128 for Dr.GRPO), 3 epochs SFT / 1 epoch DPO / 5 epochs GRPO, reward = binary execution-match (R=1) with a 0.2-weighted predicate-Jaccard partial-credit fallback for non-executable code (§B.5, Table: Training hyperparameters) -- useful precedent for reward-shaping if c22-style checkers were ever used inside an RL loop rather than pure eval.
quotes: "we introduce \deontic, a benchmark of 6,232 tasks across U.S. federal taxes, airline baggage policies, U.S. immigration administration, and U.S. state housing law" (Abstract).

"Besides free-form chain-of-thought reasoning, DeonticBench enables an optional solver-based workflow in which models translate statutes and case facts into executable Prolog, leading to formal problem interpretations and an explicit program trace." (Abstract).

"We evaluate three prompting strategies: Direct, Zero-shot, and Few-shot. Direct prompting predicts the final answer without generating Prolog. Zero-shot prompting generates a complete executable Prolog program from statutes and case facts under domain-specific output constraints for automatic execution and scoring." (§3.1).
"Stronger frontier models (e.g., GPT-5.1, O3, Claude Sonnet 4.5) perform best overall, but none is consistently best across domains or settings." (§4.1; verbatim-checked at line 303 of source).

"Across all the domains, a large fraction of errors arise from incorrect answers rather than abstentions... when solving tasks via Prolog, models exhibit higher abstention rates, whereas direct prompting reduces abstentions but increases the frequency of incorrect predictions." (§4.1).

"rule selection errors dominate in legally complex settings such as Housing and USCIS-AAO... entity and fact extraction errors are particularly prominent in SARA tasks, especially under zero-shot prompting." (§4.3).

"Compared with RuleArena and CL-bench, DeonticBench is larger (6,232 vs. 816 tasks in RuleArena), covers additional high-stakes legal domains, and provides executable Prolog artifacts for program-trace analysis." (§5.1).
"Although training improves Prolog generation quality, current RL methods still fail to solve these tasks reliably." (Abstract).

corrections: protocol.observation_content and relation_to_candidate.position both overgeneralized the per-instance observation as always containing 'statute + case text + question' across all domains. Corrected: Housing (5,314 of 6,232 tasks, 85% of the benchmark) has NO narrative case description at all -- per §E.5, 'Unlike the other three domains, Housing instances contain no narrative case description -- each instance is a (state, statutes, question) triple,' and the Housing Zero-Shot prompt template (§B.4) takes only state/focus_year/question/statutes fields, with no case-facts section. Only SARA Numeric/Binary, Airline, and USCIS-AAO include a narrative case text.

top-level 'claim' field asserted '44.4% accuracy on SARA Numeric and 46.6 macro-F1 on Housing across 8 general-purpose LLMs and 3 coding models' as if these were the true maxima across all 11 evaluated models. Corrected: these are the Abstract's own figures (O3 Zero-Shot 44.4 and GPT-4.1 Few-Shot 46.6), drawn from the 8-general-LLM main table, but within that same table GPT-5.1 Few-Shot actually scores higher on Housing (46.8), and the separately-tabulated coding model GPT-5.2-Codex scores higher on SARA Numeric (45.8, Appendix C.1) -- so the Abstract's 'best...only' framing is not literally the maximum across all evaluated models; the dossier now notes this explicitly instead of restating it as fact.

Claude Sonnet 4.5 model entry claimed its Direct-mode SARA Numeric score of 41.2 was 'second-highest.' Corrected to 'third-highest' -- O3 (44.4, Zero-Shot) and GPT-5.1 (44.0, Zero-Shot) both score higher among the general-purpose LLMs.

baseline_relevant 'Floors/ceilings observed' entry stated 'best Housing is 46.6 (GPT-4.1, Few-Shot)' without qualification. Corrected to note GPT-5.1 Few-Shot actually scores higher (46.8) in the same main-results table, and that 46.6 is specifically the figure the Abstract cites, not the true table maximum.

O3 model entry's parenthetical '44.4 (Zero-Shot -- highest SARA Numeric result reported)' was ambiguous/overbroad. Clarified to specify this is the highest among the 8 general-purpose LLMs and the Abstract's cited figure, while GPT-5.2-Codex (a coding model, reported separately in Appendix C.1) scores higher at 45.8.
